en.extract:
The pink-necked green pigeon (Treron vernans) is a species of bird of the pigeon and dove family, Columbidae. It is a common species of Southeast Asia, found from Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam south through to the major islands of Indonesia and the Philippines (where it is called "punay"). It is a medium-sized pigeon with predominantly green plumage; only the male has the pink neck that gives the species its name. The species lives in a wide range of forested and human-modified habitats and is particularly found in open habitats. Its diet is dominated by fruit, in particular figs. Pairs lay two eggs in a flimsy twig nest in a tree, shrub, or hedge, and work together to incubate the eggs and raise the chicks. The species is thought to be an important disperser of fruit seeds. The species has adapted well to human changes to the environment, and can be found in crowded cities as long as fruiting trees are present.


Taxonomy
Carl Linnaeus described the pink-necked green pigeon as Columba vernans in 1771. Its specific name, vernans, is derived from the Latin word vernantis  for "brilliant" or "flourishing". It was later moved to the green pigeon genus Treron.  Within that genus the species is most closely related to the similar looking orange-breasted green pigeon of India and Southeast Asia. The species has had up to nine subspecies described, along with the nominate race, but among the important ornithological checklists the International Ornithological Congress' (IOC) Birds of the World: Recommended English Names, the Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World  and The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World do not accept any described subspecies as valid and all treat the species as monotypic. Only the Handbook of the Birds of the World's HBW Alive lists any subspecies, with the proviso that the difference between them is in many cases clinal and further research is necessary to determine if any of them are valid.
"Pink-necked green pigeon" has been designated as the official common name for the species by the IOC. It is also known as the pink-necked pigeon.


Description
The pink-necked green pigeon is a medium-sized pigeon, measuring 25 to 30 cm (9.8–11.8 in) in length and weighing around 105–160 g (3.7–5.6 oz). The species has  sexually dimorphic plumage. The male has a grey head, pinkish neck and upper breast, and the rest of the breast is orange. The back is olive green and the wings are green with black primaries and yellow edging on the tertiaries which create a yellow bar across the wing in flight. The belly is yellowish with grey flanks, and the tail is grey with a black band at the end, and a chestnut uppertail coverts. The female is smaller overall, has a yellowish belly, throat and face, and greenish crown and back of the neck, although is otherwise similar to the male. The legs are pink or reddish, and the bill is white, pale blue green or grey. Juvenile birds look similar to females but are greyer above.
Pigeons in the genus Treron are unusual in the family for not having cooing calls, instead making whistling and quacking noises, but some cooing notes have been recorded for the pink-necked green pigeon, as the male makes a tri-syballic whistling call ending in a coo. It is also reported to make a rasping krrak krrak... call, but the species is generally held to not be particularly vocal, usually only calling in communal roosts and when it finds food.


Distribution and habitat
The range of the pink-necked green pigeon extends from southern Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam south through the Malay Peninsula and across the Greater Sundas (and their surrounding islands), Bali, Lombok, Sumbawa, and as far east as the Moluccas as well as the Philippines. It occupies a variety of habitats, including primary forest, forest edge, secondary forest, and coastal mangroves. It favours more open environments and where it is found in association with denser forest it is typically on the edges. It is also readily found in human dominated environments such as gardens, plantations and farmland. It is more common in lowlands and close to the coast, but can be found up to 300 m (980 ft) in the Philippines, 750 m (2,460 ft) in Borneo and 1,200 m (3,900 ft) in Sulawesi. The species is recorded as non-migratory by the Handbook of the Birds of the World, but other sources have described it as making local movements. A related species, the thick-billed green pigeon, covers vast distances in search of fruit, and it is likely that the pink-necked green pigeon has a similar behaviour.
After the main island of Krakatoa was obliterated in a volcanic eruption in 1883, leaving a handful of smaller islands, the pink-necked pigeon was observed on the first bird survey of these remnants. The survey was conducted in 1908, and at the time the pigeon was the only obligate frugivore (meaning it ate mostly fruit, as opposed to as part of a wider diet or opportunistically) that had established itself on the islands. Within the archipelago it was able to colonise Anak Krakatau, a volcano that emerged from the sea from the caldera in 1927, within 36 years of the new island suffering a large eruption in 1952. The delay between the island settling down and colonisation was likely due to the time taken for figs to become established on the island and begin fruiting. It later became extinct on that island, due to a small population and predation. The species has recently expanded its range, having colonised Flores at some time since 2000.


Behaviour

The pink-necked green pigeon is primarily a frugivore, taking a range of fruits, particularly figs (Ficus). Fruit of other trees are taken as well, including Glochidion, Breynia, Vitex, Macaranga, Muntingia, Melastoma, Oncosperma and Bridelia. Shoots, buds and seeds are also taken, but much less commonly so, often by quite a substantial margin. In one study of the frugivores of Sulawesi 55 observations were made of this species feeding and every one was of it eating fruit, mostly figs. The species feeds in the mid-canopy of the forest and rarely feeds in the understory or on the ground. It is described as being agile when clinging on fine branches to reach fruits at the end. Like other members of the genus Treron, the gizzard is muscular and contains grit, which is used to grind and digest seeds inside fruit. Studies of closely related species have found that not every individual has grit, and it is likely the same is true of this species. It is social, feeding in small groups or, where an abundant source of food is found, quite large flocks of up to 70 birds. The species also roosts communally, and can form roosting flocks of hundreds of birds.
There is no defined breeding season and it has been recorded breeding all year across its range. The task of building the nest is divided by sex, with the male being responsible for collecting the nesting material and the female building it. The nest itself is a simple and flimsy platform of twigs and finer material. Two eggs are laid, which are white and measure 26.8 mm–28.9 mm × 20.3 mm–21.8 mm (1.06 in–1.14 in × 0.80 in–0.86 in). The nest is placed in a tree, shrub or hedge, and can be quite close to the ground, ranging from 1 to 10 m (3.3–32.8 ft). The breeding biology of this species is virtually unknown, with only a single breeding report from Singapore. In that report, the pair shared incubation duties, with the male incubating during the day and the female at night, with the incubation time being 17 days. On hatching the chicks are brooded continuously for the first few days of life, as with incubation the male broods during the day and the female at night. Chicks are near-naked and have brown skin with a few white pin feathers on hatching. Chicks leave the nest 10 days after hatching, but remain in the nesting area for a few days after hatching, and continue to be fed by their parents.


Ecology

Like many fruit-eating pigeons, the pink-necked green pigeon is thought to be an important disperser of fruit seeds in forests and woodlands. The grinding gizzard was thought to mean the species was entirely a seed predator instead of a seed disperser but studies of closely related species have shown that not every bird crop contains grinding stones and some seeds could pass through, and the same is likely to be true of this species. The species is thought to be one of those responsible for helping the return of many of the Ficus species to the islands of Krakatoa after the obliteration of the original island in a volcanic eruption. It may not have been responsible for the first shrubby fig species, which may have been carried by generalists such as bulbuls, but once some fruiting figs had established on the island it could have been responsible for both bringing new species of Ficus to the islands and then moving the seeds between the islands. Its flight time to the islands of Krakatoa has been estimated at 48 minutes, far shorter than the estimated seed retention time in its gut of 60–480 minutes.
The pink-necked pigeon has been reported being preyed upon by white-bellied sea-eagles, and peregrine falcons have been implicated in the localised extinction of the species on Anak Krakatau.


Status
An adaptable species, T. vernans has fared well with human-made changes to its range. It has readily moved into cities and is common in Singapore's protected areas and even its gardens, and has become more common over time. In spite of suffering some hunting pressure in Thailand, Malaysia and Sumatra, and being targeted by the cage bird trade, it remains common there and across most of its range. Because it is not considered to be in any danger of extinction it has been evaluated as least concern by the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.


References


External links

Pink Necked Green Pigeon - Pigeonpedia

The '''pink-necked green pigeon''' ('''''Treron vernans''''') is a species of bird of the pigeon and dove family, [[Columbidae]]. It is a common species of [[Southeast Asia]], found from [[Myanmar]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]] south through to the major islands of [[Indonesia]] and the [[Philippines]] (where it is called "punay"). It is a medium-sized pigeon with predominantly green [[plumage]]; only the male has the pink neck that gives the species its name. The species lives in a wide range of forested and human-modified habitats and is particularly found in open habitats. Its diet is dominated by [[fruit]], in particular [[fig]]s. Pairs lay two eggs in a flimsy twig nest in a tree, shrub, or hedge, and work together to incubate the eggs and raise the chicks. The species is thought to be an important [[Seed dispersal#Animals: epi- and endozoochory|disperser of fruit seeds]]. The species has adapted well to human changes to the environment, and can be found in crowded cities as long as fruiting trees are present.

==Taxonomy==
[[Carl Linnaeus]] described the pink-necked green pigeon as ''Columba vernans'' in 1771.<ref name="Linnaeus, 1771"/> Its specific name, ''vernans'', is derived from the [[Latin]] word ''vernantis '' for "brilliant" or "flourishing".<ref name="Jobling">{{cite web|last1=Jobling|first1=J. A.|title=Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology|url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/key-to-scientific-names-in-ornithology?name=vernans|website=Handbook of the Birds of the World|publisher=Lynx Edicions, Barcelona|access-date=21 February 2019|language=en|date=2018}}</ref> It was later moved to the green pigeon [[genus]] ''[[Treron]]''.  Within that genus the species is most closely related to the similar looking [[orange-breasted green pigeon]] of India and Southeast Asia. The species has had up to nine subspecies described, along with the nominate race, but among the important ornithological checklists the [[International Ornithological Congress]]' (IOC) ''[[Birds of the World: Recommended English Names]]'', the [[Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World]]  and [[The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World]] do not accept any described subspecies as valid and all treat the species as monotypic.<ref name="ioclist"/><ref name="howard">{{cite web |last1=Dickinson |first1=Edward |last2=Christidis |first2=Les |title=The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World version 4.0 (Downloadable checklist) |url=https://www.howardandmoore.org |website=The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World |access-date=23 February 2019 |date=2014}}</ref><ref name="clements">{{cite web |last1=Clements |first1=J |last2=Schulenberg |first2=T |last3=Iliff |first3=M |last4=Roberson |first4=D |last5=Fredericks |first5=T |last6=Sullivan |first6=B |last7=Wood |first7=C |title=The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2018 |url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ |website=The Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=19 February 2019 |date=2018}}</ref> Only the ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]''{{'}}s HBW Alive lists any subspecies, with the proviso that the difference between them is in many cases clinal and further research is necessary to determine if any of them are valid.<ref name="hbw" />

"Pink-necked green pigeon" has been designated as the official common name for the species by the IOC.<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=Pigeons | work=World Bird List Version 9.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pigeons/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=20 February 2019}}</ref> It is also known as the pink-necked pigeon.<ref name="hbw" />

==Description==
The pink-necked green pigeon is a medium-sized pigeon, measuring {{convert|25|to(-)|30|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weighing around {{convert|105|-|160|g|abbr=on}}. The species has  sexually dimorphic [[plumage]]. The male has a grey head, pinkish neck and upper breast, and the rest of the breast is orange. The back is [[Olive-green|olive green]] and the wings are green with black {{birdgloss|primaries}} and yellow edging on the {{birdgloss|tertiaries}} which create a yellow bar across the wing in flight. The belly is yellowish with grey flanks, and the tail is grey with a black band at the end, and a [[Chestnut (color)|chestnut]] {{birdgloss|uppertail coverts}}. The female is smaller overall, has a yellowish belly, throat and face, and greenish crown and back of the neck, although is otherwise similar to the male. The legs are pink or reddish, and the bill is white, pale blue green or grey. Juvenile birds look similar to females but are greyer above.<ref name="hbw">{{cite web | last1=Baptista | first1=L. |last2=Trail | first2=P. |last3=Horblit | first3=H. |last4=Kirwan | first4=G. M. |last5=Garcia | first5=E. |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |year=2019 |title= Pink-necked Green-pigeon (''Treron vernans'') |work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=https://www.hbw.com/species/pink-necked-green-pigeon-treron-vernans |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |access-date=20 February 2019 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Pigeons in the genus ''[[Treron]]'' are unusual in the family for not having cooing [[bird vocalisation|calls]], instead making whistling and [[quacking]] noises,<ref name="hbw family"/> but some cooing notes have been recorded for the pink-necked green pigeon, as the male makes a tri-syballic whistling call ending in a coo.<ref name="hbw"/> It is also reported to make a rasping ''krrak krrak...'' call,<ref name="Robson"/> but the species is generally held to not be particularly vocal, usually only calling in communal roosts and when it finds food.<ref name="hbw"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The range of the pink-necked green pigeon extends from southern Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam south through the [[Malay Peninsula]] and across the [[Greater Sundas]] (and their surrounding islands), [[Bali]], [[Lombok]], [[Sumbawa]], and as far east as the [[Moluccas]] as well as the Philippines. It occupies a variety of habitats, including [[primary forest]], [[forest edge]], [[secondary forest]], and coastal [[mangrove]]s.<ref name="hbw" /> It favours more open environments and where it is found in association with denser forest it is typically on the edges.<ref name="myers" >{{cite book |last1=Myers |first1=Susan |title=Birds of Borneo: Brunei, Sabah, Sarawak, and Kalimantan |date=2009 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton and Oxford |isbn=978-0-691-14350-7 |page=97}}</ref> It is also readily found in human dominated environments such as gardens, plantations and farmland. It is more common in lowlands and close to the coast, but can be found up to {{convert|300|m|ft|abbr=on}} in the Philippines,<ref name="hbw" /> {{convert|750|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Borneo<ref name="myers" /> and {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in [[Sulawesi]]. The species is recorded as [[bird migration|non-migratory]] by the ''Handbook of the Birds of the World'',<ref name="hbw" /> but other sources have described it as making local movements.<ref name="Robson">{{cite book |last1=Robson |first1=Craig |title=Birds of Southeast Asia: Thailand, peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Vietnam, Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar |date=2005 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton and Oxford |isbn=978-0-691-12435-3 |page=80}}</ref> A related species, the [[thick-billed green pigeon]], covers vast distances in search of fruit, and it is likely that the pink-necked green pigeon has a similar behaviour.<ref name="Thornton 1996"/>

After the main island of [[Krakatoa]] was obliterated in a [[1883 eruption of Krakatoa|volcanic eruption in 1883]], leaving a handful of [[Krakatoa Archipelago|smaller islands]], the pink-necked pigeon was observed on the first bird survey of these remnants. The survey was conducted in 1908, and at the time the pigeon was the only obligate [[frugivore]] (meaning it ate mostly fruit, as opposed to as part of a wider diet or opportunistically) that had established itself on the islands.<ref name="Thornton 1996">{{cite journal |last1=Thornton |first1=Ian W. B. |last2=Compton |first2=Stephen G. |last3=Wilson |first3=Craig N. |title=The role of animals in the colonization of the Krakatau Islands by fig trees (''Ficus'' species) |journal=Journal of Biogeography |date=1996 |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=577–592 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2699.1996.tb00019.x|bibcode=1996JBiog..23..577T }}</ref><ref name="Whittaker 1994">{{cite journal |last1=Whittaker |first1=Robert J. |last2=Jones |first2=Stephen H. |title=The Role of Frugivorous Bats and Birds in the Rebuilding of a Tropical Forest Ecosystem, Krakatau, Indonesia |journal=Journal of Biogeography |date=1994 |volume=21 |issue=3 |page=245 |doi=10.2307/2845528|jstor=2845528 |bibcode=1994JBiog..21..245W |url=https://zenodo.org/record/8205074 }}</ref> Within the archipelago it was able to colonise [[Anak Krakatau]], a volcano that emerged from the sea from the [[caldera]] in 1927, within 36 years of the new island suffering a large eruption in 1952.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Thornton |first1=I. W. |last2=Zann |first2=R. A. |last3=Rawlinson |first3=P. A. |last4=Tidemann |first4=C. R. |last5=Adikerana |first5=A. S. |last6=Widjoya |first6=A. H. |title=Colonization of the Krakatau Islands by vertebrates: equilibrium, succession, and possible delayed extinction. |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=1 January 1988 |volume=85 |issue=2 |pages=515–518 |doi=10.1073/pnas.85.2.515 |pmid=3422440 |pmc=279581 |bibcode=1988PNAS...85..515T |doi-access=free }}</ref> The delay between the island settling down and colonisation was likely due to the time taken for figs to [[Ecological succession|become established on the island]] and begin fruiting.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Compton |first1=S. G. |last2=Thornton |first2=I. W. B. |last3=New |first3=T. R. |last4=Underhill |first4=L. |title=The Colonization of the Krakatau Islands by Fig Wasps and Other Chalcids (Hymenoptera, Chalcidoidea) |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |date=19 December 1988 |volume=322 |issue=1211 |pages=459–470 |doi=10.1098/rstb.1988.0138 |bibcode=1988RSPTB.322..459C |doi-access= }}</ref> It later became extinct on that island, due to a small population and predation.<ref name="falcon">{{cite journal |last1=Thornton |first1=Ian |title=Figs, frugivores and falcons: an aspect of the assembly of mixed tropical forest on the emergent volcanic island, Anak Krakatau |journal=South Australian Geographical Journal |date=1994 |volume=93 |pages=3–21}}</ref> The species has recently expanded its range, having [[Colonisation (biology)|colonised]] [[Flores]] at some time since 2000.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schellekens |first1=Mark |last2=Trainor |first2=Colin |last3=Encallado |first3=Juan |last4=Imansyah |first4=M Jeri |title=Status of the Pied Imperial Pigeon ''Ducula bicolour'' and Pink-necked Green-Pigeon ''Treron vernans'' on Flores, Nusa Tenggara |journal=Kukila |date=2009 |volume=14 |pages=16–20 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/235688039}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
[[File:Pink-necked Green-Pigeon - juveniles - Flickr - Lip Kee.jpg|thumb|left|Recently fledged chicks]]
The pink-necked green pigeon is primarily a [[frugivore]], taking a range of fruits, particularly figs (''Ficus''). Fruit of other trees are taken as well, including ''[[Glochidion]]'', ''[[Breynia]]'', ''[[Vitex]]'', ''[[Macaranga]]'', ''[[Muntingia]]'', ''[[Melastoma]]'',<ref name="hbw" /> ''[[Oncosperma]]'' and ''[[Bridelia]]''.<ref name="Whittaker 1994"/> Shoots, buds and seeds are also taken, but much less commonly so, often by quite a substantial margin. In one study of the frugivores of [[Sulawesi]] 55 observations were made of this species feeding and every one was of it eating fruit, mostly figs.<ref name="walker" >{{cite journal |last1=Walker |first1=J. S. |title=Dietary specialization and fruit availability among frugivorous birds on Sulawesi |journal=Ibis |date=2007 |volume=149 |issue=2 |pages=345–356 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2006.00637.x |bibcode=2007Ibis..149..345W }}</ref> The species feeds in the mid-canopy of the forest and rarely feeds in the understory or on the ground. It is described as being agile when clinging on fine branches to reach fruits at the end.<ref name="hbw" /> Like other members of the genus ''Treron'', the [[gizzard]] is muscular and contains grit, which is used to grind and digest seeds inside fruit.<ref name="hbw family">{{cite web | last1=Baptista | first1=L. |last2=Trail | first2=P. |last3=Horblit | first3=H. |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |year=2019 |title= Pigeons, Doves (Columbidae) |work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=https://www.hbw.com/family/pigeons-doves-columbidae |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |access-date=February 26, 2019|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Studies of closely related species have found that not every individual has grit, and it is likely the same is true of this species.<ref name="Thornton 1996"/> It is social, feeding in small groups or, where an abundant source of food is found, quite large flocks of up to 70 birds. The species also roosts communally, and can form roosting flocks of hundreds of birds.<ref name="hbw" />

There is no defined [[breeding season]] and it has been recorded breeding all year across its range. The task of building the nest is divided by sex, with the male being responsible for collecting the nesting material and the female building it. The nest itself is a simple and flimsy platform of [[twig]]s and finer material. Two eggs are laid, which are white and measure {{convert|26.8|-|28.9|x|20.3|-|21.8|mm|abbr=on}}. The nest is placed in a tree, [[shrub]] or [[hedge]], and can be quite close to the ground, ranging from {{convert|1|to(-)|10|m|ft|abbr=on}}. The breeding biology of this species is virtually unknown,<ref name="hbw" /> with only a single breeding report from Singapore. In that report, the pair shared incubation duties, with the male incubating during the day and the female at night, with the incubation time being 17 days. On hatching the chicks are brooded continuously for the first few days of life, as with incubation the male broods during the day and the female at night. Chicks are near-naked and have brown skin with a few white [[pin feather]]s on hatching. Chicks leave the nest 10 days after hatching, but remain in the nesting area for a few days after hatching, and continue to be fed by their parents.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wee |first1=Y. C. |title=Forging a closer relationship with Pink-necked Green-pigeons |journal=Nature Watch |date=2005 |volume=13 |issue=3 |pages=16–21 |url=https://www.nss.org.sg/articles/f0766cb6-eForgingaCloserRelationship.pdf |archive-date=2022-03-30 |access-date=2019-02-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220330042657/https://www.nss.org.sg/articles/f0766cb6-eForgingaCloserRelationship.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref>

==Ecology==
[[File:Treron vernans - Lip Kee.jpg|thumb|upright|Male on [[Rakata]] in Krakatoa]]
Like many fruit-eating pigeons, the pink-necked green pigeon is thought to be an important [[Seed dispersal#Animals: epi- and endozoochory|disperser of fruit seeds]] in forests and woodlands. The grinding gizzard was thought to mean the species was entirely a seed predator instead of a seed disperser but studies of closely related species have shown that not every bird crop contains grinding stones and some seeds could pass through, and the same is likely to be true of this species. The species is thought to be one of those responsible for helping the return of many of the ''Ficus'' species to the islands of Krakatoa after the obliteration of the original island in a volcanic eruption. It may not have been responsible for the first shrubby fig species, which may have been carried by generalists such as [[bulbul]]s, but once some fruiting figs had established on the island it could have been responsible for both bringing new species of ''Ficus'' to the islands and then moving the seeds between the islands. Its flight time to the islands of Krakatoa has been estimated at 48 minutes, far shorter than the estimated seed retention time in its gut of 60–480 minutes.<ref name="Thornton 1996"/>

The pink-necked pigeon has been reported being preyed upon by [[white-bellied sea-eagle]]s, and [[peregrine falcon]]s have been implicated in the localised extinction of the species on Anak Krakatau.<ref name="Rawlinson">{{cite journal |last1=Rawlinson |first1=P |last2=Zann |first2=R |last3=van Balen |first3=S |last4=Thornton |first4=I |title=Colonization of the Krakatau Islands by Vertebrates |journal=GeoJournal |date=1992 |volume=28 |issue=2, Krakatau – a Century of Change |pages=225–231|doi=10.1007/BF00177236 |bibcode=1992GeoJo..28..225R |s2cid=189887589 }}</ref>

==Status==
An adaptable species, ''T. vernans'' has fared well with human-made changes to its range. It has readily moved into cities and is common in Singapore's protected areas and even its gardens,<ref name="hbw" /> and has become more common over time.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=KwekYan |first1=C |last2=SiYang |first2=T |last3=Kurukulasuriya |first3=B |last4=YiFei |first4=C |last5=Rajathurai |first5=L |last6=Lim |first6=H |last7=Tan |first7=H |title=Decadal changes in urban bird abundance in Singapore. |journal=Raffles Bulletin of Zoology |date=2012 |volume=Supplement 25 |pages=89–196 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/256094237}}</ref> In spite of suffering some hunting pressure in Thailand, Malaysia and Sumatra,<ref name="hbw" /> and being targeted by the [[wildlife trade|cage bird trade]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shepherd |first1=Chris |title=The bird trade in Medan, north Sumatra: an overview |journal=BirdingASIA |date=2006 |volume=5 |pages=16–24 |url=https://www.traffic.org/site/assets/files/5655/bird-trade-medan.pdf }}</ref> it remains common there and across most of its range.<ref name="hbw" /> Because it is not considered to be in any danger of extinction it has been evaluated as [[least concern]] by the [[IUCN Red List of Threatened Species]].<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" />
{{Clear}}

zh.extract:
红颈绿鸠（学名：Treron vernans）是鸠鸽科绿鸠属的一种。分布于东南亚，包括柬埔寨、印度尼西亚、汶莱、马来西亚、缅甸、菲律宾、新加坡、泰国和越南等。它们的自然栖息地是热带及副热带低地雨林、红树林和山地森林。
